text.title: graph-rag-retrieval Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules every application of graph-rag-retrieval enforces: classify each query, route by type, start hybrid, cap neighbor expansion, and fall back safely.

rule.statement: Classify every query before retrieval using one fast LLM call (haiku/gpt-4o-mini, temperature=0) into the four canonical types: GLOBAL, ENTITY, RELATIONSHIP, LOCAL. On classification failure, fall back to LOCAL.
rule.rationale: Without classification, either all retrieval paths run (3-5x latency) or one strategy degrades accuracy for wrong-type queries. A single cheap call routes each query to its best-fit strategy.

rule.statement: For GLOBAL queries, always read the pre-computed hierarchical/community summary — never trigger live graph traversal at query time.
rule.rationale: Full-graph traversal for global answers takes seconds-to-tens-of-seconds on graphs >50k nodes. Pre-computed summaries serve them in milliseconds; that is the entire point of offline summarisation.

rule.statement: For RELATIONSHIP queries, wrap every shortest-path call in try/except for NetworkXNoPath and NodeNotFound, and cap hop depth at 3.
rule.rationale: Disconnected subgraphs are common; an uncaught NetworkXNoPath crashes retrieval for valid relationship queries. Unbounded depth turns a dense graph into exponential path enumeration.

rule.statement: Start with hybrid retrieval (vector candidates → graph neighbor expansion → dedupe by chunk-id) before committing to pure graph-only retrieval. Limit neighbor expansion to ≤3 neighbors per entity.
rule.rationale: Pure graph retrieval without a vector anchor surfaces structurally connected but semantically irrelevant chunks. Unbounded expansion fills the context window with low-relevance entity chunks.

rule.statement: When ENTITY/RELATIONSHIP paths fail (entity not in graph, no extractable entities, no path), fall back to LOCAL vector search with k=5 before returning empty context. Never return an empty answer when fallback is available.
rule.rationale: A failed graph lookup is not the same as no relevant context — vector search frequently has the answer the graph route missed. Always preserve the fallback path for retrieval continuity.

rule.statement: If no knowledge graph exists or all queries are local/chunk-answerable, skip graph-rag-retrieval and use plain vector + reranking instead.
rule.rationale: Routing overhead and graph-traversal cost are unjustified when the graph is missing or unused.
